glossary.def: An implicit generative model trained as a two-player game: a generator turns noise into samples while a discriminator learns to tell those fakes from real data. The generator never sees real data directly — it learns only from the gradient flowing back through its opponent, a learned and moving loss function.

The adversary network. In the original GAN it outputs a probability D(x) in (0,1) that x is real; in WGAN it becomes a 1-Lipschitz critic outputting an unbounded real score. At the game's optimum D(x)=0.5 for every input — it can no longer distinguish real from generated.

The single value function the two networks optimize in opposite directions: the discriminator maximizes it (scoring real high, fake low) while the generator minimizes it. With the optimal discriminator substituted in, the generator is provably minimizing the Jensen–Shannon divergence between the data and its samples.

A structural failure where the generator maps many latent codes to a few (or one) outputs, fooling the current discriminator without covering the full data distribution. Because the bare adversarial objective contains no term rewarding diversity, dropping a mode entirely scores as well as covering it.

Goodfellow's practical reformulation in which the generator maximizes log D(G(z)) instead of minimizing log(1−D(G(z))). It shares the same fixed point but supplies strong gradients early in training, when the discriminator easily rejects the generator's outputs and the original loss is nearly flat.

The minimum average 'dirt times distance' cost of reshaping one probability distribution into another. Unlike Jensen–Shannon divergence it varies continuously even when the two distributions have disjoint support, so it provides a usable gradient where JSD is stuck at a flat ceiling — the basis of WGAN.

GAN variants that replace the JS-divergence objective with the Wasserstein-1 distance via its Kantorovich–Rubinstein dual, turning the discriminator into a 1-Lipschitz critic. The Lipschitz constraint is enforced by weight clipping (WGAN) or, more stably, a gradient penalty (WGAN-GP), giving a loss that correlates with sample quality.

A style-based generator that maps the latent z to an intermediate code w and uses it to modulate feature-map statistics (adaptive instance normalization) at every resolution. Coarse layers control pose and shape, fine layers control texture, enabling disentangled style mixing and the first widely photorealistic synthetic faces.

refs.cite: Goodfellow, I. et al. (2014). Generative Adversarial Networks. NeurIPS 2014.
Radford, A., Metz, L. & Chintala, S. (2015). Unsupervised Representation Learning with Deep Convolutional Generative Adversarial Networks (DCGAN). ICLR 2016.
Arjovsky, M., Chintala, S. & Bottou, L. (2017). Wasserstein GAN. ICML 2017.
Gulrajani, I. et al. (2017). Improved Training of Wasserstein GANs (WGAN-GP). NeurIPS 2017.
Karras, T., Laine, S. & Aila, T. (2019). A Style-Based Generator Architecture for GANs (StyleGAN). CVPR 2019.
Salimans, T. et al. (2016). Improved Techniques for Training GANs. NeurIPS 2016.
Dhariwal, P. & Nichol, A. (2021). Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis. NeurIPS 2021.
